title: The effect of female leadership on contracting from Capitol Hill to Main Street
abstract: This paper provides novel evidence that female politicians increase the proportion of US government procurement contracts allocated to women-owned firms. For identification, we use a regression discontinuity design on a sample of mixed-gender elections in the US House of Representatives. The effect grows over a female representative's tenure and concentrates in female representatives who are on powerful congressional committees. Changes in the pool of and behavior by government contractors cannot explain the result. The more gender-balanced representation in government contracting is not associated with economic costs.

author.Jonathan Brogaard*: David Eccles School of Business, University of Utah, United States of America
author.Nataliya Gerasimova: Department of Finance, Norwegian Business School, Norway
author.Maximilian Rohrer: Department of Finance, Norwegian School of Economics, Norway

article.1. Gender contracting gap, institutional: 1.1. Gender contracting gapSince the first federally sponsored National Women's Conference in November 1977, the federal government has consistently acknowledged that WOBs confront significant institutional barriers and discrimination in the United States (see Sirmons (2004)). As Brush (1998) pointed out, female business owners had to work harder than men to convince “government procurement officials and corporate customers that they are as competent as men.” In focus groups investigating the source of the gap, female business owners articulated concerns of not being taken seriously as business owners (see Brush (1998)).One response to the shortfall of WOBs in procurement has been the development of preferential procurement programs, whereby government agencies explicitly seek to increase their purchases of goods and services from WOBs. In 2000, Congress enacted a set-aside program for Women-Owned Small Businesses in the Equity in Contracting for Women Act.20 Regardless, it took ten years for the SBA to issue its final rule for the program, and the program only took effect in 2011.Another push for equality is the 2014 law that allowed WOBs to qualify for no-bid “sole source” contracts. Federal officials have discretion to award “sole source” contracts to a single qualified small business after negotiating terms, thus avoiding prolonged bidding with multiple companies. Senator Jeanne Shaheen, Democrat of New Hampshire, sponsored this law based on needs of female contractors such as Sue Sylvester. Ms. Sylvester stated that this law would provide “an opportunity to get […] experience and get ready to compete” for the more numerous and larger contracts.21In 2016, economists at the US Department of Commerce submitted a report on female representation in government contracting that states: “the odds of businesses owned by women winning a federal contract are about 21 percent lower than for otherwise similar companies, and years of effort to increase those chances have barely made an impact, according to a new report from the Commerce Department.”22An even more recent survey analysis by Goldman Sachs 10,000 Small Businesses Voice reports that 99% of WOBs believe the federal government could do more to support their businesses.23 89% said the playing field for female-owned small businesses is not level compared to male-owned small businesses.1.2. Institutional detailsFederal procurement contracts are agreements between government agencies and businesses to obtain goods and services for a negotiated price and length.The process of awarding government contracts starts with a federal agency identifying a need for a purchase of a good or service. The agency decides the degree and method by which the contract is competed, the form of pricing appropriate to the contract, and whether the contract is for a definitive quantity or some indefinite delivery vehicle (IDV). Then, a contracting officer of this agency posts a solicitation on the SAM.gov website. A solicitation announcement identifies what the agency wants to buy, provides instructions to potential contractors, identifies the source-selection method to evaluate offers, and includes a deadline for the submission of bids or proposals. Firms then submit their offers for review by agency officers who evaluate them and make the final decision.24Firms interested in getting a federal contract must obtain a Dun & Bradstreet Data Universal Numbering System (DUNS) unique number for each of the business's physical locations and register with the federal government's System for Award Management (SAM). Firms that identify themselves as a small business in the federal government's System for Award Management (SAM) must meet the Small Business Act's definition of a small business and must not exceed the size standards established and periodically updated by the SBA. The Small Business Act defines a small business as one that is organized for profit; has a place of business in the United States; operates primarily within the United States or makes a significant contribution to the US economy through payment of taxes or use of American products, materials, or labor; is independently owned and operated; and is not dominant in its field on a national basis. The business may be a sole proprietorship, partnership, corporation, or any other legal form.Until the summer of 2020, small businesses could self-certify as a WOB when they registered at SAM. For the identification of WOBs among contractors, we utilize a data item named “Women-Owned Business.” As defined by the Small Business Act, a women-owned small business is one that is a small business, with at least 51% ownership and control by U.S. citizen women, where women manage day-to-day operations and make long-term decisions. There is no additional monetary or time cost for firms to choose the women-owned small business flag in the system. In addition, self-misclassification is punished. The SBA has implemented several measures to strengthen the “eligibility examination procedures” for small businesses, including requiring adequate documentation to prove small business status. These measures take place to ensure that ineligible businesses do not take advantage of small business set-asides. The SBA also has enforcement measures; for example, the SBA has the right to bar large contractors who identify themselves as a women-owned small business fraudulently from participating in federal procurement opportunities (McManus, 2012). The SBA has the right to make unannounced site visits. Also, a contracting officer or third party is allowed to appeal the SBA's finding of eligibility by filing a “status protest” with the Office of Hearing and Appeals (McManus, 2012).We focus on SBA set-aside contracts with specified terms and conditions, so-called definitive contracts (DCs), for two main reasons. First, 94% of women-owned firms are small businesses according to the Commerce Department (Beede and Rubinovitz, 2015). Second, DCs allow us to assess how female representatives impact government allocation in stand-alone, one-time agreements with a single firm for the purchase of goods or services under specified terms and conditions. In contrast, IDVs are contracts with one or more firms, and at the time of the contract award there is uncertainty about the quantity of goods or services to be provided, the timing of delivery, or the scope of the agreement. This initial uncertainty might affect the tractability of the contract performance measures.25The SBA set-aside is the most prevalent set-aside program. Around 20% of all government contracts and 27% of contracting dollars are awarded through the SBA set-aside program.26 It sets aside federal procurement opportunities for small business concerns, regardless of the owner's gender.27 The main requirement is that federal agencies generally reserve contracts that have an anticipated value greater than the micro-purchase threshold ($10,000 in 2020), but not greater than the simplified acquisition threshold ($250,000 in 2020), exclusively for small businesses unless the contracting officer is unable to obtain offers from two or more small businesses that are competitive with market prices and the quality and delivery of the purchased goods or services. In addition, federal agencies generally set aside contracts that have an anticipated value exceeding the simplified acquisition threshold exclusively for small businesses when there is a reasonable expectation that offers will be obtained from at least two responsible small businesses offering the products of different small businesses (Rule of Two) and the award will be made at a fair market price.1.3. DataWe rely on two main types of data: government procurement contracts and congressional election outcomes.We obtain the full sample of procurement contracts between the US government and individual firms from the FPDS-NG. This dataset is at the contract level and offers detailed information on the contract itself, the contractor, and the specific government entities participating in the procurement. In total, there are over 300 characteristics such as small business or WOB statuses.28 We limit the sample to SBA set-aside contracts that are awarded competitively and for which only small businesses can compete. We also exclude contracts that do not contain a well-defined completion date and budget. Additionally, we require the contract to be awarded directly to a firm, meaning that the contract is not awarded to the parent company and is not part of a bundled project, that the contract is awarded and performed inside the US without any foreign funding, that the contractor is neither a government organization nor a nonprofit organization, and that the contractor is registered in the US and is not foreign owned.The main dependent variable in this paper is whether a contract is awarded to a WOB. Fig. 1 presents a time series plot of the share of government contracts awarded to WOBs from 2005 to 2020. After an initial increase before 2010, the share of contracts awarded to female representatives oscillates around 20%, with a recent uptick in 2020.29Download: Download high-res image (112KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. In addition to the limits based on contracts' and contractors' characteristics, we consider all contracts that can be linked to a congressional election. Contracts are assigned to a given congressional representative if the contract was awarded between the term start and subsequent election date, and executed in the respective district.30 The sample consists of 970,968 unique contracts initially worth on average $41,000, amounting to $39 billion. The average contract awarded to WOBs (non-WOBs) amounts to $35,000 ($42,000) and lasts for around 130 days. Approximately five firms place a bid. From a performance perspective, 8.8% of contracts have a cost overrun of 4.2% on average. Around 13% of contracts have a time overrun.The last two columns report the difference and the associated standard errors clustering on year and two-digit product and service code accounting for repeated interactions. For almost all variables, the differences between WOBs and non-WOBs are statistically significant. However, for most measures, these differences are economically minor. The average characteristics are economically similar. Some of the differences can be explained by an increasing time trend in female representation and contract size over time.In the Internet Appendix, Fig. IA1 depicts the geographical spread of contract allocation to WOBs. Alabama, South Carolina, and Virginia are the states with the highest share of contracts allocated to WOBs: more than 26%. Fig. IA2 depicts the individual two-digit product and service codes with the highest and lowest contract allocation to WOBs. The figure suggests that service-related products are more likely to be procured by WOBs, while R&D-related contracts are more likely to be procured by non-WOBs. In Table IA1, we investigate characteristics of individual contractors, WOBs and non-WOBs, separately for female and male representative districts. Disregarding the gender of the district representatives, WOBs tend to be roughly six years younger, have lower revenue, and have fewer employees, significant at the 1% level.The second main input in our analysis is female political representation. The number of women in politics in general has been increasing over time, long before the start of the sample. article.2. Congresswomen and women-owned busines: This section tests the effect of electing a female representative on government contract allocation to WOBs. We start with a naïve regression before moving to an RDD for causal inference. The results show causal evidence that female representatives lead to more WOBs being allocated government contracts. The results withstand a variety of validity and robustness checks.2.1. Ordinary least squares specificationWe start by investigating correlations between the gender of a district representative and the probability of a government contract being awarded to a WOB. We estimate the following model:(1)WOBc,t,d=α+β1×Female representativet,d+Xc,t,d′γ+ηFE+ϵc,t,d in which WOBc,t,d is an indicator variable that equals one if a contract c initiated at time t and performed in a congressional district d is awarded to a women-owned firm, and zero otherwise. Female representativet,d is an indicator variable that equals one if the congressional district d at time t is represented by a female legislator.Xc,t,d′ is a vector of control variables and γ the vector of respective coefficients. WOBs tend to be smaller than the average firms and hence might have a harder time competing for bigger and longer-maturity contracts. However, the final contract terms are part of the bid submitted by the individual contractor winning the contract and subsequent negotiation. In other words, the final contract size and maturity can be viewed as outcomes and hence are bad controls. We opt for including indicator variables for different contract size and maturity bins, instead of continuous measures. By including bins, we aim at alleviating the bad control problem but still accounting for structural differences in ability to compete for certain contracts faced by WOBs.To address potential differences between male and female politicians, we add age and political affiliation as controls. Next, to account for the fact that politicians from the ruling party might enjoy more political power to pursue their policies, we add three indicator variables equaling one if the politician is aligned with the House majority, Senate majority, and the president. We also include an indicator variable for special elections. Lastly, we include district output (GDP) as a proxy for economic activity. Not including firm controls is a deliberate choice as they are outcomes of the selection process.35Lastly, we include a host of fixed effects, ηFE. We include three-digit product and service code fixed effects, which capture 927 different individual categories of products and services procured by the government. We also include fixed effects for congressional districts, months, and the 165 unique subagencies that award contracts. The granularity of the data allows us to include a variety of stringent fixed effects. We include awarding agency × year fixed effects, two-digit product and service code × year fixed effects, and state × year fixed effects. The multidimensional nature of fixed effects allows us to isolate a very specific source of variation, namely variation in the gender of the politician representing the district.The main coefficient of interest is β1, which measures how much more likely it is that a contract is allocated to a WOB in congressional districts with female representatives. Table 3 presents the results. Throughout the paper we use linear probability models rather than logit or probit because probabilistic models are biased in the presence of a large number of fixed effects (Maddala, 1987; Greene, 2004). We cluster standard errors at election and awarding agency. This is because the propensity to assign contracts to a given type of firm, such as WOBs, might correlate on this dimension. An example of why this could be is agencies repeatedly purchasing similar products during a given time period.Table 3. Unconditional regression. This effect is significant at the 1% level. Including the high dimensional interaction fixed effects slightly increases the main coefficient to 2.9. The unconditional probability of a WOB receiving a contract in the regression sample is 20%, which implies that the difference between districts with female versus male representatives amounts to more than 13% of its unconditional mean. These results are only indicative as the inference might be subject to endogeneity bias. To address endogeneity concerns we implement the RDD.2.2. Regression discontinuity designThe main challenge of identifying the causal effect of legislator gender on WOB contract allocation is that US House representatives are not randomly assigned to different congressional districts. A naïve regression analysis of the government contractor's gender on the gender of the district representative might be biased because of unobserved characteristics correlating with both the dependent and independent variables. For example, female empowerment or the Me Too movement might simultaneously drive female involvement in the political arena as well as in business.One solution to addressing endogeneity concerns in this setting is to use the RDD on a sample of mixed-gender elections.36 We follow the design by Ferreira and Gyourko (2014), Brollo and Troiano (2016), and Hasan et al. (2020) closely. Similar to us, those studies identify the effect of female US politicians (mayors) on political and policy outcomes, and on municipal bond pricing.37 Because of the ex-ante uncertainty regarding the outcomes at the threshold, this approach provides quasi-random variation in election winners, implying that the gender of the winning candidate is arguably determined by pure chance.We estimate the causal effects of female political representatives on government contracting as follows:(2)WOBc,t,d=α+β×Female representativet,d+P(θ,Vote margint,d)+Xc,t,d′γ+ηFE+ϵc,t,dThe assignment variable Vote margint,d is defined as the difference in vote percentage obtained by the female minus the male candidate. For example, if the male candidate received 40% of the votes and the female 37%, the vote margin would amount to -3%. P stands for a nth-order polynomial in the vote margin, potentially interacted with an indicator variable of the female candidate winning, to control for different functional forms. θ is the vector of the respective coefficients. To increase the precision of the estimator of the treatment effect, similar to Ferreira and Gyourko (2014) and Hasan et al. (2020), we also include predetermined control variables as well as fixed effects; see Equation (1) Section 2.1 for the exact list. Because congressional district fixed effects subsume many election outcomes, we use state fixed effects for RDD regressions.For the main part of the paper, we conduct local linear regressions with a bandwidth of h=30%. In other words, our main sample consists of all mixed-gender elections with a vote margin in the range of [−30%,30%]. We use a relatively wide bandwidth so that we can control for omitted variables using higher-order fixed effects. When we repeat the analysis using tighter bandwidths, some fixed effects subsume the individual election outcomes.Nevertheless, we do show that our results are robust to varying the bandwidth from 5% to 75%.38 In comparison, Ferreira and Gyourko (2014) use all mixed-gender mayoral elections while Hasan et al. (2020) and Brollo and Troiano (2016) select the bandwidth following Imbens and Kalyanaraman (2012) with a linear control function of the assignment variable without controls. Using the same methodology yields a bandwidth of 5.88% in our setup.39 Ferreira and Gyourko (2014), Brollo and Troiano (2016), and Hasan et al. (2020) all focus on mixed-gender elections, but study elections at a more granular level, namely municipalities or cities. We focus on federal elections to the House of Representatives, which yields a substantially smaller set of potential elections to study.Table 4 reports the main results. For all specifications, the coefficient of interest is positive and statistically significant at the 1% level. Models 1 to 3 differ in how we control for the forcing variable: first-order polynomial, first-order polynomial interacted with an indicator variable capturing a female win, and second-order polynomial, respectively. Models 4 and 5 gradually add higher-level fixed effects such as awarding agency interacted with year, product and service code interacted with year, and state interacted with year. These fixed effects capture shifts in procurement on agency, product, and state level over time, such as WOBs becoming the primary contractors for different products at a given time. Because of this, these fixed effects help to rule out stories that deal with structural shifts of procurement as an explanation, such as the idea that women-led districts only acquire products that WOBs offer. representative increases the probability that a government contract is awarded to a WOB between 4.1 to 7.1 percentage points, depending on the specification. For the remainder of the paper, we use Model 3 and hence the discussion of economic magnitude is based on the corresponding estimate of 4.5 percentage points. Throughout the paper, we refer to the estimates of the coefficients as percentage points, rather than percentages, to avoid confusion. The linear probability model predicts a linear increase in probabilities and not percentage changes.From 2005 to 2020, the share of SBA contracts allocated to WOBs increased from 16.5% to 27.5%. The effect of a single female representative, 4.5 percentage points, is roughly a third of the overall time trend.40To gauge the relative importance of this effect for the entire government contracting system, we provide a back-of-the-envelope calculation. In 2020, the government procured $665 billion.41 There are 435 congressional districts, implying that each district allocates contracts worth $1.5 billion. Our estimate suggests that the election of a single female representative implies a shift of $0.07 billion (4.5 percentage points, see Table 4, Model 3). In the 116th Congress, 101 women serve as House representatives. Hence, the estimate suggests a transfer of around $6.9 billion from non-WOBs to WOBs for the whole of the US. This back-of-the-envelope calculation clearly relies on simplifying assumptions. In the Internet Appendix, we provide two pieces of evidence justifying our extrapolation. First, on average, more than 75% of government contracts are awarded to small businesses.42 Second, the effect of female representation on contract allocation does not depend on firm size as measured by revenue and number of employees.Next, we discuss the economic relevance for a single firm in our sample. The average government contractor received 4.4 contracts under the SBA set-aside program.43 The median firm receiving government contracts has annual revenue of $2.1 million and 12 employees. 4% of revenue is due to government purchases. The median WOB is smaller, with $1.1 million in annual revenue and 7.5 employees. A considerably bigger share of their revenue, 9%, is due to government purchases. For 26% of WOBs in the sample, government contracts under the SBA program amounted to more than 50% of their revenue.Taken together, government contracts are important both to the contracting firms as well as to the overall economy. While the long-term effects of government contracts on WOB are beyond the scope of this paper, Hvide and Meling (2023) suggest that being rewarded a government contract causally increases growth for start-ups even in the long run after the contract ends. If our findings extend to all government procurement, the economic impacts are a magnitude of order larger.2.3. ValidityIn this subsection, we present the evidence on the validity of our research design. In particular, we conduct a McCrary (2008) test, we report the absence of discontinuities for 20 potential district-level confounders, and we show that neither time nor political affiliation can predict election outcomes.2.3.1. Political affiliation of the female candidate and time trendsThe causal interpretation of our results hinges on the assumption that election outcomes are not predictable. One might worry about two particular confounders: first, the number of women in politics is increasing over time (see Fig. 2) and hence the probability of women winning elections might be due to an unobserved time trend. Second, female candidates running for office tend to be Democrats (around 73%), which might affect their probability of winning the election. We explicitly test if either variable can predict election outcomes. We estimate a regression predicting the gender of the winning candidate with an indicator variable equaling one if the female candidate is a Democrat and a set of indicator variables for each individual Congress. Table A2 in the Appendix shows the results separately for all elections, elections with a vote margin of 30% or lower, and elections with a vote margin of 5% or lower (Models 1 to 3). The results suggest that election outcomes are not predictable by the time trend or the political orientation of the female legislator.452.4. RobustnessIn this subsection, we present evidence that our results are robust to perturbations of our empirical approach. First, we conduct placebo regressions randomizing election outcomes. Second, we show that the main result is robust to the choice of bandwidth and to removing individual states from the sample. Third, we show that the results are robust to the gender of the heads of federal agencies.2.4.1. Placebo regressionAnother concern is that the effect is not due to the final treatment, whether or not a female legislator represents the district, but due to some unobserved variables correlated with the assignment variable, the female vote margin. For example, districts that are more likely to elect a female legislator might have more female government contractors. Whether or not the female legislator is elected at the margin may not matter. To rule out this possibility, we implement placebo regressions as a falsification test. For each iteration, we use our main sample and randomize the election outcomes, female versus male winner. All other variables are kept constant. If our result is due to treatment, one would not expect to find any results in the placebo regressions. We run 10,000 regressions identical to Model 3 in Table 4 and report a histogram of the main coefficient, as displayed in Fig. A4. 99.9% of the obtained placebo coefficients are lower than our estimate (indicated by a red dashed line in the figure), suggesting that the main effect is due to treatment, the legislator's gender.2.4.2. Varying bandwidthFor the main analysis, we use a bandwidth of 30%. To alleviate concerns that the results are sensitive to the threshold, we perform the main analysis for different choices of bandwidth, considering elections with a vote margin ranging from 5% to 75%.46Table 5 shows that the results are not sensitive to the specific bandwidth. The statistical significance on the female representative variable remains at or above the 5% level for all specifications. The coefficient magnitude on the female representative variable increases from 3.0 percentage points to 5.1 percentage points as the bandwidth increases. For bandwidths below 15%, we use a slightly less strict specification. In particular, we replace fixed effects for congressional districts with fixed effects for individual states because the former would subsume many individual election outcomes. We also do not control for a second-order polynomial of the forcing variable.Table 5. Robustness – varying bandwidth. Gender of government agency headsLastly, we explore whether the effect is driven by female legislators shifting government contracts to agencies that are led by women, and that female-led agencies are more likely to respond to the representative's preferences. That is, it may be the gender of the agency leader that explains the allocation of contracts to WOBs, not the politician's gender. We manually gather data on the gender of government agency and subagency heads from their websites. We augment our original specification with an interaction term, testing whether the gender of the agency head acts as a magnifier. The results are reported in Table A3 in the Appendix.We do not observe evidence that female-led agencies award more contracts to women than other agencies do in the presence of female legislators. If anything, female-led agencies award fewer contracts to women. In our sample, agencies are rarely led by women. Only 8% (4%) of contracts were awarded by agencies (subagencies) with a female head. These results are consistent with Fernandez et al. (2013), who find no evidence that a higher level of female representation in federal agencies leads to more contracts for WOBs.
article.3. Mechanism: The previous section established that electing a female House representative causes an increase in government contracts allocated to WOBs. Next, we examine two potential underlying mechanisms: a supply-side mechanism and a demand-side mechanism. In the supply-side mechanism, the presence of female representatives changes the behavior or the pool of WOBs. This leads to more contract allocation to WOBs despite the government not changing the contract-allocation process. In the demand-side mechanism, the government actively allocates more contracts to WOBs due to the female representative while firms do not change their behavior. The mechanisms need not to be mutually exclusive. Ideally, one would like to study a setting in which the government is “treated” with a female politician while the composition of firms in the economy is unchanged. Such an ideal setting does not exist.3.1. Supply-side mechanismWe start by investigating whether our main effect is driven by changes in the pool of potential government contractors and in their behavior, a supply-side mechanism. The supply-side mechanism argument is that female representatives do not change the government contract decisions, but that the mere presence of a female representative improves the environment for women in business and their willingness to bid on government contracts. The female congressional presence may also inspire women to create businesses that later become government contractors. Such a role-model effect is documented by Chizema et al. (2015) and Kedia and Pareek (2023), who show that female political representation affects board composition.One straightforward test to analyze the supply-side mechanism would be to examine how contract bidding changed after the election of female representatives. Unfortunately, the data does not allow us to observe the identity of all bidders for a given contract. We conduct three tests investigating the pool of contractors, bidding behavior, and short-term effects. First, we show that the election of female legislators does not cause a change in the pool of contractors in general, their reallocation to female-led districts, or changes in their status as WOBs. Second, we show that the election of a female legislator does not increase the number of bidders for government contracts. Third, we show that the main effect persists even in short windows such as 180 days after the start of a female legislator's term, and in such short windows it is unlikely that the pool of contractors can change. For reference, an average company will not sign its first contract with the Department of Defense until 18 months after registration. While we cannot directly study the pool of applicants for each individual contract, the three results presented in this section are inconsistent with the supply-side mechanism.3.1.1. Pool of contractorsWe start by investigating whether the election of a female representative affects the pool of government contractors. If our main effect was driven by the supply-side mechanism, we expect that the election of a female legislator would lead to the following changes: more WOBs registered in her district, more new WOBs as potential contractors in her district, more WOBs reallocating from other districts to her district, or more firms reclassifying as WOBs in her district. These potential findings would be consistent with the idea of firms changing their behavior in response to the election of a woman. We use historical snapshots of government contractors, which is free of survivorship bias and captures all potential contractors, not only the firms that got at least one contract.Empirically, we estimate the following pooled ordinary least squares (OLS) regression:(3)Ye,t,d=α+β×Female representativee,t,d+Xe,t,d′γ+ηFE+ϵe,t,dObservations are per election e in congressional district d at time t. We consider three additional measures that capture whether firms changed their registration information in response to the representative's gender: the share of WOBs that were previously registered as non-WOBs, the share of firms that were registered somewhere else, and the share of WOBs that were registered somewhere else.Table 6 shows the results.47 For all models, the coefficient of interest, female representative, is statistically insignificant and quantitatively close to zero. The results suggest that the election of female representatives does not change the pool of contractors. This analysis also addresses the concern of firms' self-misclassification because of perceived benefits associated with a legislator's gender. If firms engaged in this behavior, we would expect an increase in firms classifying themselves as WOBs in response to the election of a female legislator (Model 5) or WOBs changing their location to a district run by a female legislator (Model 7). Thus, we infer that the main effect is driven by the demand-side mechanism: the government actively allocating more contracts to WOBs because of the female representative while firms do not change their behavior.In a contemporaneous working paper, Krause and Sevilir (2023) document that the election of a female mayor has a positive effect on female start-up activity. In the Internet Appendix, Section IA.2, we reconcile this evidence with our finding that female representatives have no effect on the pool of government contractors. First, we show that there is little overlap with the start-up ecosystem Krause and Sevilir (2023) analyze. Second, we show that the number of start-ups does not meaningfully change in the relatively short horizons around the elections that we consider.3.2. Dynamics of the demand-side mechanismWe turn our analysis to studying the dynamics of the demand-side mechanism. First, we explore the persistence of the main effect: Does the increased allocation of contracts to WOBs persist if the female legislator is replaced by a man? Conversely, is there a difference between confirming a female incumbent versus electing a new female successor? Second, we test whether the main effect concentrates in female legislators with oversight power of government agencies, which speaks to how female legislators affect government contract allocation. We end this section with an analysis of local networks and political affiliation.3.2.1. PersistenceDoes temporary representation by a female legislator have a persistent effect? Minority groups might suffer from decision-makers' inattention to them and initial beliefs that they will perform worse (Bartos et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2023).48 Exposure to minority contractors might permanently modify contracting. On the other hand, evidence that the loss of political connections between firms and legislators affects contract allocation and contract terms (Tahoun, 2014; Brogaard et al., 2021) suggests that the effects might not persist beyond the legislators' time in office.We deviate slightly from our main regression approach because focusing on mixed-gender elections with a vote margin of 30% or less does not take into account that the election of a female candidate might affect who will run for office going forward. For example, imagine that the election of a female legislator increases the probability that the losing party will put forward a female candidate in the next election. In this case, the successive elections would not be in our sample. In Model 3, we directly test tenure and confirm that it is an economically and statistically significant contributor to WOBs being awarded government contracts.These results cannot speak to whether a male replacing a female wipes out the initial increase due to the female representative. The main reason is that both the initial election and the reelection of a female representative have a positive effect on government contract allocation to WOBs. In the Internet Appendix, Section IA.3, we provide an analysis of past female representation. In line with the result of this section, there is a positive effect to past female representation.3.2.2. Oversight through committeesGovernment purchases are procured by federal agencies, which are subject to oversight by congressional committees. If changes in contract allocation are due to individual legislators, we hypothesize a larger effect if the legislator is part of a powerful congressional committee. This mechanism would be consistent with our previous finding that the effect increases over time for a given individual female legislator because participation and seniority in committees is tied to an individual and increases with tenure.Congressional oversight refers to the review, monitoring, and supervision of federal agencies, programs, and policy implementation, and it provides the legislative branch with an opportunity to inspect, examine, review, and check the executive branch and its agencies. The authority of Congress to provide oversight is derived from its implied powers in the US Constitution, various laws, and House rules.51Prior literature has identified specific committees that are particularly important in affecting government spending. Edwards and Stewart (2006) identify “power committees” in both the Senate and House of Representatives. In the House, these committees are Ways and Means, Appropriations, Energy and Commerce, Rules, Foreign Affairs, Armed Services, Intelligence, Judiciary, Homeland Security, and Transportation and Infrastructure. Cohen et al. (2011) document the causal influence these committees have on government spending. Using similar procurement data as we do, Brogaard et al. (2021) document that members of Congress affect contract renegotiations through the power of the purse (budgetary committees) and the power of oversight (oversight committees). Local networksIn this section, we explore whether the main effect can be explained by local networks. In related work, Schoenherr (2019) finds that Lee Myung Bak, the Korean president elected in 2007, disrupted the allocation of government contracts to private firms by appointing CEOs from his own networks to lead state-owned enterprises. We proxy local networks following Hunt (2020), who argues that local roots signify specific ties to a district that precede a representative's tenure in Congress. These ties offer practical advantages, including enhanced name recognition, access to local political and economic networks, and opportunities for campaign communication. This unique perspective enables representatives to forge connections with business owners in the district (see Hunt (2021)).52In our empirical analysis, we augment our main model with three variables that capture local networks, as outlined by Hunt (2022): whether the representative was born, went to high school, and had a business in the congressional district.53 The findings are presented in Table A4 in the Appendix. Model 1 confirms the robustness of our primary effect, female representatives increasing the allocation of government contracts to WOBs, within a more restricted sample of representatives for which we observe local networks. If networks enhance the main effect, we anticipate a positive and statistically significant estimate for the interaction of local networks and female representative. However, our analysis does not yield supporting evidence.3.2.4. Political affiliationNext, we explore heterogeneity associated with the party of the elected representative. Table 10 reports estimates of our main specification augmented with an indicator variable equaling one if the representative is Republican. The impact of female representatives on government contract allocation is bigger, both statistically and economically, when the female representative is affiliated with the Republican party. For example, Senator Jeanne Shaheen, Democrat from New Hampshire, says, “Women entrepreneurs may be the country's greatest untapped economic resource.”54 However, their emphasis on specific issues may vary. Generally, Republicans tend to prioritize business-related topics; see Witko and Friedman (2008). For example, Republicans stated in their 2012 platform that “[s]mall businesses are the leaders in the world's advances in technology and innovation, and we pledge to strengthen that role and foster small business entrepreneurship.”55 Taken together, female representatives from both parties contribute to increased contract allocation to WOBs, with female Republican representatives exerting a more substantial influence.The argument above is in line with survey evidence on the political orientation of small business owners. Almost 50% of small business owners identify as Republican or have Republican political leanings.56 Republicans are often regarded by small business owners in general, which includes female small business owners, as more suitable representatives of the needs of small businesses. These owners believe that their firms would fare better under Republican governance.57To sum up, this section studies the dynamics of the demand-side mechanism. First, the effect grows over the tenure of an individual female legislator and decreases if she is replaced by a male legislator. Second, the effect is stronger with female legislators who are on powerful congressional committees. Networks cannot explain the effect. Lastly, Republican female representatives have a bigger effect on contract allocation to WOBs than their Democratic female peers.
article.4. Contract and firm outcomes: 4.1. Contract terms and executionThis subsection addresses whether changes in government contractor composition affect the costs and quality of government procurement. If female politicians reduce a preexisting and value-destroying gender bias in government contracting, this reallocation will benefit the US government because contract quality will improve. However, if female politicians lead to more contracts to WOBs rather than to the best-bidding firm, contract quality will deteriorate. It is also possible that the quality distribution among bidders at the margin is fairly uniform such that they are largely interchangeable. If so, the allocation to WOBs versus non-WOBs will not impact contract quality.4.1.1. Contract termsWe first investigate whether the election of a female politician relates to contractor-favorable initial terms. We estimate Equation (4) using four initial contract terms as dependent variables: contract size, maturity, whether a firm is exempt from providing cost or pricing data, and whether a contract is a multiyear deal (for more than one but less than five years). The last two variables are often referred to as “sweetheart” terms.58 All other variables are as defined in Section 2.1, with the exception that we omit contract amount and maturity from the vector of controls Xc,t,d. Firm outcomesHvide and Meling (2023) document that receiving government contracts has a causal and long-term effect on firms. They focus on Norwegian Public Roads Administration procurement auctions between 2003 and 2015, for which they observe the identity of firms that win the auction as well as the ones that narrowly lose. Their main results are that firms that win a procurement auction have 20% higher revenue and employment even several years after the original procurement auction. This suggests that we should expect that access to government contracts for WOBs has a long-term effect.Establishing a causal effect is challenging in this context since we lack direct observations of applicants. article.5. Conclusion: This paper provides novel evidence that female political representation causes an increase in government contracts allocated to WOBs. For identification, we exploit mixed-gender elections and the RDD.The results are driven by a demand-side mechanism: female legislators alter government procurement and firms do not change their behavior. We show that the main effect grows over the representative's tenure and that it concentrates in female legislators who are part of congressional committees. In contrast, changes in the supply of government contractors do not explain the results. We do not observe significant economic costs associated with more gender-balanced representation in contracting.Taken together, we provide evidence that the gender gap can be narrowed by exposure to leaders from the groups that experience bias. Female representatives have a causal impact on WOBs receiving government contracts. In the case of government contract allocation, the effect of female representation is economically large and can play a role similar to that of programs dedicated to helping women-owned firms. That being said, there is still a disparity in the representation of WOBs and non-WOBs.
